text: This video demonstrates a robotic redo ileocolic resection with takedown of duodenal fistula in complex Crohn's disease. Our patient is a sixty-three year old female with a forty year history of Crohn's disease. She underwent a prior ileocolic resection in nineteen eighty-two as well as other abdominal procedures. She had been untreated with medications until the past five years. She has since been refractory to medical therapy and has developed obstructive symptoms with subsequent malnutrition. CT scan imaging demonstrates an anastomotic stricture with a fistula to the duodenum. On colonoscopy, she was noted to have an impassable colonic stricture at seventy centimeters, the site of her prior resection. This image demonstrates the prior laparotomy incision. In addition is the configuration of the robotic trocars. There are three eight millimeter trocars placed in the lower abdomen, one twelve millimeter stapling port in the left mid abdomen, and an insufflating assistant port in the left upper quadrant.

The robotic arms are docked, and the surgeon performs extensive adhesiolysis using a vessel sealing device, revealing the anastomotic stricture and a dilated proximal bowel. The thickened and inflamed mesentery of the ileocolic anastomosis is evaluated. A window is created in the small intestine's mesentery, and the bowel is transected with two blue load stapler firings. A similar window is made in the colon's mesentery, allowing for its transection with two staple firings. The anastomosis is freed and rolled caudally, exposing the fistula from the ileocolic anastomosis to the duodenum. The duodenum is visible to the right, and the fistula is separated using the vessel sealing device, revealing the mucosa of the anastomosis and residual tissue on the duodenum's serosa.

The surgeon uses sharp dissection and electrocautery to clean the tissue around the duodenum, revealing the remaining defect. A 2-0 barbed suture is selected and used in a running fashion to close the defect in the duodenal wall. After closure, the duodenum is submerged under saline, and endoscopy is performed to check the patency, showing an intact closure without air leak.

The surgeon creates a new ileocolic anastomosis, checking perfusion with indocyanine green. Two O-sutures are used to tack the colon to the small intestine for an isoparastaltic anastomosis. Three stay sutures are placed to align the bowel side by side. Aneurotomies are made in the small intestine and colon using hot shears, and a 60mm blue load stapler is passed through to create the common channel. The stapler is removed, and a two-oh barbed suture is used in a running fashion to close the common defect, ensuring the crotch of the anastomosis is closed. A drain is placed through a trocar site and secured with a nylon suture. The specimen is extracted through an extended 12mm trocar site using an endocatch bag.
A photograph shows the patient's abdominal incisions three weeks post-operative, with no visible signs of infection or complications.
